research.interface: Jira Cloud platform REST API v3 issue attachments (multipart/form-data Add attachment)

official_sources.title: The Jira Cloud platform REST API — official OpenAPI v3 specification
official_sources.used_for: Add attachment: POST /rest/api/3/issue/{issueIdOrKey}/attachments, 'Attachments are posted as multipart/form-data (RFC 1867)', 'The request must have a X-Atlassian-Token: no-check header, if not it is blocked', 'The name of the multipart/form-data parameter that contains the attachments must be file'; 200 response is a JSON ARRAY of attachment beans {id, self, filename, author, created, size, mimeType, content, thumbnail}; 403/404 error cases and 413 'the attachments exceed the maximum attachment size for issues... more than 60 files are requested to be uploaded... the per-issue limit for attachments has been breached'

official_sources.title: The Jira Cloud platform REST API — About (v3 intro)
official_sources.used_for: Special headers: 'Operations that accept multipart/form-data must include the X-Atlassian-Token: no-check header in requests. Otherwise the request is blocked by cross-site request forgery (CSRF/XSRF) protection.'

official_sources.title: Basic auth for REST APIs — Atlassian Developer
official_sources.used_for: Authorization: Basic base64(email:api_token) — the Atlassian account email joined with an API token by a colon, base64-encoded; API tokens instead of passwords

research.language_rationale: The build/release tooling this client joins is C#, and Atlassian ships no official .NET SDK for Jira Cloud — the documented integration surface is REST, and the catalog item pins wire-level multipart behavior (the exact form parameter name, the XSRF bypass header, per-part filename/content-type preservation, the array-shaped partial response) that HttpClient/MultipartFormDataContent expresses directly. .NET 10 with System.Text.Json is the idiomatic zero-extra-dependency stack.

verified_facts: Add attachment is POST /rest/api/3/issue/{issueIdOrKey}/attachments with a multipart/form-data body (RFC 1867); one or more files travel in one request.
The request must carry X-Atlassian-Token: no-check or it is blocked by CSRF/XSRF protection — this applies to every multipart/form-data operation in the API.
The multipart form parameter name for every attached file must be exactly 'file'; each part carries its own filename and content type, which Jira preserves as the attachment's filename and mimeType.

A successful upload answers 200 with a JSON array of attachment beans ({id, self, filename, author, created, size, mimeType, content, thumbnail}); the array covers the files that were stored, so a response with fewer entries than files sent means the absent files did not make it and must be reported, not ignored.

413 is the documented over-limit failure: attachment size over the instance maximum, more than 60 files in one request, or the per-issue attachment limit breached; other failures use the standard ErrorCollection {errorMessages, errors}.
The attachment bean's content field is a download URL; fetching it can redirect to a separate media host, and basic credentials must not be forwarded to a host other than the Jira base.
Authentication is HTTP basic: base64 of email:api_token in the Authorization header.
